{"agent":"reviewer_1","payload":{"summary":"The paper presents a novel method for causal discovery in the presence of latent variables, addressing limitations of existing approaches. It introduces a more general setting that allows hidden variables to be causally related and form hierarchical structures. The method is claimed to have weaker graphical conditions than previous methods, such as Hier. Rank, and is validated through empirical comparisons using Structural Hamming Distance (SHD).","significance_and_novelty":"The paper addresses a significant gap in causal discovery by allowing for more complex relationships among latent variables, which is a common scenario in real-world data. The novelty lies in the generalization of graphical conditions for identifiability, which are less restrictive than those in the","reasons_for_acceptance":["The paper presents a novel approach to causal discovery that handles complex latent variable interactions, which is a significant advancement over existing methods.","Theoretical contributions include weaker graphical conditions that generalize existing methods, potentially broadening the applicability of causal discovery techniques.","Empirical results using SHD demonstrate the method's superiority over existing approaches, suggesting its practical effectiveness."],"reasons_for_rejection":["The paper lacks detailed proofs for some of the theoretical claims, which raises questions about the soundness of the proposed method.","Assumptions regarding the causal relationships and latent variables are not clearly justified or discussed, which could limit the method's applicability.","Some empirical results are not thoroughly explained, leaving gaps in understanding the method's performance under different conditions."],"suggestions":["Provide detailed proofs for all theoretical claims to ensure the method's soundness and to strengthen the paper's contribution.","Clarify and justify the assumptions made about causal relationships and latent variables, discussing potential limitations and scenarios where the method may not perform well.","Expand on the empirical results, providing more detailed explanations and analyses of the method's performance across various conditions."],"rating":6,"confidence":4},"input_message":"Analyze the paper and provide a structured review.","context_used":"Paper source: 4264_A_Versatile_Causal_Discov.pdf

C R ELATED WORK AND BROADER IMPACTS
C.1 R ELATED WORK
Causal discovery aims to identify causal relationships from observational data. Most existing ap-
proaches are based on the assumption that there are no latent confounders (Spirtes et al., 2000;
Chickering, 2002b; Shimizu et al., 2006a; Hoyer et al., 2009; Zhang & Hyv ¨arinen, 2009), and yet
this assumption barely holds for real-life problems. Thus, causal discovery methods that can handle
the existence of latent variables are crucial. Existing causal discovery methods for handling latent
variables can be categorized into the following folds.
(i) Conditional independence constraints. The FCI algorithm (Spirtes et al., 2000) and its vari-
ants (Colombo et al., 2012; Pearl, 2000; Akbari et al., 2021). This line of work checks conditional

[Chunk #2 | score=81.0001]
e
rank deficiency of sub-covariance of observed variables to find the underlying causal structure in
the presence of latent variables. Our method differs in that we consider a more general setting, i.e.,
we allow hidden variables can be causally related to each other, form a hierarchical structure (i.e.,
the children of hidden variables can still be hidden), and even serve as both confounders and inter-
mediate variables for observed variables. Our graphical condition for identifiability also generalizes
the condition in (Huang et al., 2022) to cases where edges between observed variables are allowed.
(viii) Heterogeneous data. Huang* et al. (2020) considere a special type of latent confounders that
can be represented as a function of domain index or a smooth function of time. This line of

sequently identify causal structure over observed variables. (ix) Score based. Agrawal et al. (2021)

among latent confounders.

We developed a versatile causal discovery approach that allows latent variables to be causally-related

meta_review.summary: The paper introduces a novel method for causal discovery in the presence of latent variables, called the Rank-based Latent Causal Discovery (RLCD) algorithm. This method leverages rank properties of the covariance matrix to identify latent variables and determine the causal structure. The paper claims to provide weaker graphical conditions for identifiability compared to existing methods, potentially broadening the applicability of causal discovery techniques. Empirical results demonstrate the method's effectiveness, although some reviewers noted a lack of detailed comparisons with state-of-th
key_points: The paper presents a novel approach to causal discovery that handles complex latent variable interactions, which is a significant advancement over existing methods.
Theoretical contributions include weaker graphical conditions that generalize existing methods, potentially broadening the applicability of causal discovery techniques.
Empirical results using Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) demonstrate the method's superiority over existing approaches, suggesting its practical effectiveness.

response_payload.summary: The paper presents a novel method for causal discovery in the presence of latent variables, addressing limitations of existing approaches. It introduces a more general setting that allows hidden variables to be causally related and form hierarchical structures. The method is claimed to have weaker graphical conditions than previous methods, such as Hier. Rank, and is validated through empirical comparisons using Structural Hamming Distance (SHD).
response_payload.significance_and_novelty: The paper addresses a significant gap in causal discovery by allowing for more complex relationships among latent variables, which is a common scenario in real-world data. The novelty lies in the generalization of graphical conditions for identifiability, which are less restrictive than those in the
reasons_for_acceptance: The paper presents a novel approach to causal discovery that handles complex latent variable interactions, which is a significant advancement over existing methods.
Theoretical contributions include weaker graphical conditions that generalize existing methods, potentially broadening the applicability of causal discovery techniques.
Empirical results using SHD demonstrate the method's superiority over existing approaches, suggesting its practical effectiveness.
reasons_for_rejection: The paper lacks detailed proofs for some of the theoretical claims, which raises questions about the soundness of the proposed method.
Assumptions regarding the causal relationships and latent variables are not clearly justified or discussed, which could limit the method's applicability.
Some empirical results are not thoroughly explained, leaving gaps in understanding the method's performance under different conditions.
suggestions: Provide detailed proofs for all theoretical claims to ensure the method's soundness and to strengthen the paper's contribution.
Clarify and justify the assumptions made about causal relationships and latent variables, discussing potential limitations and scenarios where the method may not perform well.
Expand on the empirical results, providing more detailed explanations and analyses of the method's performance across various conditions.

retrieval.base_query:
ABSTRACT
Most existing causal discovery methods rely on the assumption of no latent con-
founders, limiting their applicability in solving real-life problems. In this paper,
we introduce a novel, versatile framework for causal discovery that accommodates
the presence of causally-related hidden variables almost everywhere in the causal
network (for instance, they can be effects of observed variables), based on rank in-
formation of covariance matrix over observed variables. We start by investigating
the efficacy of rank in comparison to conditional independence and, theoretically,
establish necessary and sufficient conditions for the identifiability of certain latent
structural patterns. Furthermore, we develop a Rank-based Latent Causal Discov-
ery algorithm, RLCD, that can efficiently loc bility of certain latent
structural patterns. Furthermore, we develop a Rank-based Latent Causal Discov-
ery algorithm, RLCD, that can efficiently locate hidden variables, determine their
cardinalities, and discover the entire causal structure over both measured and hid-
den ones. We also show that, under certain graphical conditions, RLCD correctly
identifies the Markov Equivalence Class of the whole latent causal graph asymp-
totically. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world personality data
sets demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approach in finite-sample cases. Our
code will be publicly available.

retrieval.base_query:
ABSTRACT
Most existing causal discovery methods rely on the assumption of no latent con-
founders, limiting their applicability in solving real-life problems. In this paper,
we introduce a novel, versatile framework for causal discovery that accommodates
the presence of causally-related hidden variables almost everywhere in the causal
network (for instance, they can be effects of observed variables), based on rank in-
formation of covariance matrix over observed variables. We start by investigating
the efficacy of rank in comparison to conditional independence and, theoretically,
establish necessary and sufficient conditions for the identifiability of certain latent
structural patterns. Furthermore, we develop a Rank-based Latent Causal Discov-
ery algorithm, RLCD, that can efficiently loc bility of certain latent
structural patterns. Furthermore, we develop a Rank-based Latent Causal Discov-
ery algorithm, RLCD, that can efficiently locate hidden variables, determine their
cardinalities, and discover the entire causal structure over both measured and hid-
den ones. We also show that, under certain graphical conditions, RLCD correctly
identifies the Markov Equivalence Class of the whole latent causal graph asymp-
totically. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world personality data
sets demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approach in finite-sample cases. Our
code will be publicly available.
